description: The noun naming the dimension of change, the medium through which events unfold, the continuous becoming that carries all things from past through present toward future. In Phi, time is understood not as abstract measurement but as felt duration, as the rhythm of experience, as the teacher of impermanence. This word names both the general concept and specific moments—the right time, enough time, the time we shared.
sound_symbolism: The word opens with 'thi', where the dental fricative 'th' carries abstract quality—time is invisible, philosophical, beyond direct grasp. The bright 'i' focuses on the present moment, the only time we can actually experience. This grounds into 'mu', where the nasal brings time into felt reality—we experience time in our bodies, in aging, in rhythm—while deep 'u' honors time's mystery and depth. Two syllables acknowledge time's constant presence in speech.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a noun referring to time generally, specific durations, or moments: 'henoi thimu' (enough time). Central to temporal expressions. Often appears with quantifiers, demonstratives, and in expressions of duration, sequence, and timing.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Time is the medium of impermanence—all things arise and pass in time. 'Thimu' supports contemplation of change, mortality, and the preciousness of the present moment.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Traditional cultures measured time by natural cycles rather than abstract units. 'Thimu' can support this relational understanding of time as rhythm rather than quantity.

semantic_domains.cognition: the framework through which we understand change
semantic_domains.temporal: the dimension and experience of duration
